title: GeneCS: Synthesizing Resource-Efficient Code Surgery for Arbitrary Quantum Stabilizer Codes
authors: Junyu Zhou, Ali Javadi-Abhari, Gushu Li
abstract: Efficiently realizing logical operations on general stabilizer codes remains a long-standing challenge in fault tolerant quantum computing. While code surgery provides a general framework with provable guarantees by joint logical measurements, existing constructions are largely theoretical and incur substantial ancilla overhead in practice. In this work, we propose GeneCS, a resource-efficient compiler for synthesizing code surgery protocols for arbitrary stabilizer codes. Our approach leverages structure-aware optimizations to eliminate redundancy in graph construction, dynamically balance expansion and congestion, and incorporate code degree constraints. Experimental results show that GeneCS achieves an average reduction of over $85\%$ in ancillary qubits and checks for both single-code and cross-code logical operations, while preserving logical error rates. Moreover, our compiler scales to codes with more than $10^4$ qubits with an amortized compilation time of about one second per instance. These results enable practical logical operations and efficient cross-code communication, thereby supporting the deployment of modern QLDPC codes and heterogeneous quantum architectures.
summary: The paper introduces GeneCS, a compiler designed to automate the creation of efficient 'code surgery' protocols for quantum error correction. By treating the construction of ancilla systems as a graph optimization problem, it significantly reduces the physical qubit overhead required for logical operations without sacrificing error protection.
main_problem: The high resource overhead (ancillary qubits and checks) required for performing fault-tolerant code surgery and cross-code communication between arbitrary stabilizer codes.
main_result: The GeneCS compiler achieves an average reduction of over 85% in ancillary qubits and checks for both single-code and cross-code operations while preserving logical error rates and scalability.
method: A graph-based synthesis framework using three optimization strategies: structure-aware graph expansion, dynamic expansion-congestion optimization, and degree-constrained optimization via graph thickening and cellulation.
model_or_system: The framework operates on quantum stabilizer codes, including topological codes (surface, color) and QLDPC codes (Bivariate Bicycle, Hypergraph Product, Quantum Tanner), modeled as low-dimensional chain complexes.
key_observables: Ancillary qubit/check overhead, code distance, logical error rate, and maximum stabilizer weight (degree).
important_parameters: Cheeger constant (expansion), congestion (edge overlap), and maximum qubit/check degrees.
main_assumptions: The scalability results observed in surface codes are assumed to extend to other CSS stabilizer codes due to their locally repetitive structure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the GeneCS workflow; Figure 2 maps surface codes to graphs; Figure 3 details graph modification; Figure 5-7 illustrate expansion, congestion, and graph thickening; Figure 9 shows logical error rate stability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the code surgery problem, defines a graph-theoretic model for ancilla systems, presents the GeneCS compiler algorithms (expansion, congestion, and degree optimization), provides experimental benchmarks on various code families, and discusses scalability and error rate performance.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
